lesson.menu_title: Block 3 -- Handling Tough Questions After the Pitch
lesson.menu_desc: Field the board's pushback after the pitch: stay composed under scrutiny, take a pressing question, and answer with the second conditional -- "If we delayed, we would lose the window" -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 16 -- Block 3 -- Handling Tough Questions After the Pitch
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Carlos Vinicius Vale Bassan | Aula 16 -- Handling Tough Questions

listenings.text: Carlos, the pitch was strong, but the pitch is not the hard part. The hard part is the next ten minutes, when the board pushes back. So let me brief you on handling tough questions under pressure. First, stay composed. A pressing question is not an attack; it is scrutiny, and scrutiny is normal. If you looked nervous, the board would doubt the case, so breathe and slow down. Second, validate before you answer. Say the question is valid, then answer it. If I were on the board, I would ask the same thing, so do not get defensive. Third, use the second conditional to stay calm and hypothetical. When they press on risk, you say: if we delayed the deal, we would lose the window. If the cost were lower, we would not even be debating it. That structure keeps your answer measured, not emotional. Fourth, add a caveat when you must, but stand by your recommendation. You can say, with one caveat, the case still holds. Do not walk it back. A defensible answer is short, calm, and tied to the numbers. If you stayed composed and answered every pressing question with a clear, defensible reason, the board would trust you. Remember: you are not defending yourself, you are defending the return.

Hi Carlos, it is Diana, calling after the Q&A session with the directors. The pitch landed, and the questions went better than last quarter, so let me give you the feedback while it is fresh. First, the good part. When the lead director put you under scrutiny about the timeline, you stayed composed. You did not rush, and your answer was defensible. That is exactly right. Second, the part to fix. On the pressing question about cost, you almost walked your recommendation back. You said maybe we could revisit the number. Do not do that. If you softened the case every time, the board would stop trusting it. Stand by the recommendation and use a caveat instead: with one caveat on timing, the case still holds. Third, the second conditional. You used it well once. When you said if we delayed, we would lose the window, the room went quiet, in a good way. Do more of that. A hypothetical answer sounds calm and senior. One more thing. When a question felt hostile, you treated the pushback as valid, and that defused it. Keep doing that. The directors said the answers were defensible, and that is the word you want. Tighten the cost answer, stay composed, and you are ready for the full board on Thursday. Strong work, Carlos. Talk soon.
